                                                                       CALENDAR OF FEATURES
                                                In addition to the monthly data appearing regularly in Employment and
                                                Earnings, special features appear in most of the issues, as shown below:
                                                                                   Jan.   Feb.   Mar.   Apr.   May   June   July   Oct.
                                      Household data
                                    Annual averages                                 x
                                    Revised seasonally adjusted series and
                                      current seasonal factors                             X
                                    Quarterly averages                              X                    X                   X       X

                                      Establishment data
                                    National annual averages:
                                      Industry divisions (preliminary)              X
                                      Industry detail (final)                                     X
                                    State and area annual averages                                              X
                                    Area definitions                                                            X
                                    National data adjusted to new benchmarks                                         (1)
                                    Revised seasonally adjusted series and
                                       current seasonal factors                                                      (1)


                                     Data shown in this issue are adjusted to March 1969 benchmarks, first published in the June
                                1970 issue. It is expected that data adjusted to March 1970 benchmarks will be introduced in the
                                September 1971 issue.

                      1
                         War veterans are defined by the dates of their service in the United States Armed Forces. War veterans 20 to 29
                years old are all veterans of the Vietnam Era (service at any time after Aug. 4, 1964), and they account for about 85
                percent of the Vietnam Era veterans of all ages. About 700,000 post-Korean-peacetime veterans 20 to 29 years old are
                not included in this table.

                the color or race of workers. The Negro and other races category,           shown in the same tables because of independent rounding of

                group were Negro; the remainder were American Indians,                      Reliability of the Estimates
                 Eskimos, Orientials, and other nonwhites. Tables in this volume
                 which contain these data utilize the word "color" to so indicate.
                                                                                                Since the estimates are based on a sample, they may differ
                                                                                             from the figures that would have been obtained if it were possible
                                                                                             to take a complete census using the same schedules and
                 ESTIMATING METHODS                                                          procedures.
                                                                                                The standard error is a measure of sampling variability, that
                     Under the estimation methods used in the CPS, all of the re-           is, the variations that might occur by chance because only a

                                                                                                 The sampling plan used in the current employment statistics
                                                                                             program is known as "sampling proportionate to average size of
                  Benchmark Adjustments                                                      establishment." This design is an optimum allocation design
                                                                                             among strata since the sampling variance is proportional to the

                                                             Seasonal Adjustments

                     Many economic statistics reflect a regularly recurring sea-           approximation based on past experience. Seasonally adjusted
                 sonal movement which can be estimated on the basis of past                estimates have a broader margin of possible error than the
                 experience. By eliminating that part of the change which can              original data on which they are based, since they are subject
                 be ascribed to usual seasonal variation, it is possible to ob-            not only to sampling and other errors but, in addition, are
                 serve the cyclical and other nonseasonal movements in the                 affected by the uncertainties of the seasonal adjustment proc-
                 series. However, in evaluating deviations from the seasonal               ess itself. Seasonally adjusted series for selected labor force
                 pattern—that is, changes in a seasonally adjusted series—                 and establishment data are published regularly in Employment
                 it is important to note that seasonal adjustment is merely an             and Earnings.
